Our findings reveal that the $R_{D/W}$ influences resource utilization efficiency and thereby affects generalization, providing insights into model shape initialization and the understanding of model generalization mechanisms. Code for Neural Interaction Law is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Neural_Interaction_Law-D788

</details>


### [425] [Geometry-Correct Diffusion Posterior Sampling with Denoiser-Pullback Curvature Guidance and Manifold-Aligned Damping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27990)
*Seunghyeok Shin,Minwoo Kim,Dabin Kim,Hongki Lim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a method for improved diffusion posterior sampling by using a per-noise-level damped Gauss–Newton correction to enhance stability and performance, achieving competitive results in image reconstruction tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve data-consistency updates in diffusion posterior sampling to prevent instability due to manually scaled guidance weights.

Method: Introduce a per-noise-level damped Gauss–Newton correction in diffusion-state coordinates that avoids the need for forward denoiser Jacobians, applying matrix-free GMRES to solve corrections, and utilizing variance-preserving Langevin transitions for sampling.

Result: Achieves competitive PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS metrics on FFHQ and ImageNet, and the best PSNR/SSIM on accelerated MRI reconstructions compared to other baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed method enhances sampling efficiency and stability while outperforming traditional approaches in key performance indicators.

Abstract: Diffusion posterior sampling conditions diffusion priors on measurements, but data-consistency updates are typically scaled by hand-tuned guidance weights and can destabilize sampling under stiff, operator-dependent curvature. We replace scalar guidance with a per-noise-level damped Gauss--Newton correction computed in diffusion-state coordinates. The correction pulls likelihood gradients back through the denoiser, uses a one-sided curvature model that avoids forward denoiser Jacobians, and applies diffusion-calibrated rank-one damping aligned with the denoiser residual. Each correction is solved with matrix-free GMRES using automatic differentiation, and sampling proceeds with a variance-preserving Langevin transition with a closed-form drift/noise split. On FFHQ and ImageNet across inverse problems, it achieves competitive PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS while running markedly faster than most of the compared baselines; on accelerated MRI reconstruction, it achieves the best PSNR/SSIM among the compared baselines.

</details>


### [426] [Teacher-Student Representational Alignment for Reinforcement Learning-Driven Imitation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28372)
*Meraj Mammadov,Pedro Zuidberg Dos Martires,Johannes Andreas Stork*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a novel algorithm for imitation learning that addresses the imitation gap caused by teacher-student isolation in reinforcement learning, using a shared embedding space to enhance student performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the imitation gap in imitation learning, where the teacher and student policies operate in isolation, leading to information discrepancies that hinder student learning.

Method: The authors introduce a shared embedding space trained through self-supervised contrastive learning, preventing the teacher policy from accessing private information, thus ensuring student policies can effectively imitate the teacher.

Result: The proposed algorithm significantly reduces the imitation gap, allowing for improved student performance in various evaluation domains when compared to state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: Using a shared embedding space facilitates better imitation learning by allowing student policies to more effectively replicate the teacher's performance without requiring a complex training setup.

Abstract: Imitation learning (IL) from a state-based reinforcement learning (RL) policy is a common approach to overcome the curse of dimensionality in complex and high-dimensional observation spaces prevalent in robotics. This paper addresses the irreducible imitation gap that emerges when teacher and student are learned in isolation, and the teacher policy has the liberty to rely on privileged state information that the student cannot infer from its observations. Instead of improving poor student performance with RL finetuning after IL, which often requires a whole new training setup, we propose a novel algorithm which learns a shared embedding space that hides agent-specific observations and thus trains imitable teacher policies by construction. We train the shared embedding space with self-supervised contrastive learning in parallel to the teacher policy and prevent it from extracting private information by limiting its gradients from updating the encoder networks. We perform evaluations on several example domains and compare to state-of-the-art baselines showing that our algorithm enables higher student performance with substantially reduced imitation gap.

</details>


### [427] [Learning Compositional Latent Structure with Vector Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28007)
*Niclas Pokel,Benjamin F. Grewe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents the Vector Network (VN), a hierarchical recurrent architecture that uses a library of reusable weight atoms to enhance compositional generalization in deep learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors seek to improve the capability of deep networks to selectively reuse computations when encountering familiar structures in novel configurations, addressing the limitation of shared weight matrices.

Method: VN utilizes a library of reusable rank-1 weight atoms instead of fixed weight matrices, minimizing layer-local energy to determine a sparse set of active weight atoms and their coefficients, which are used to create input-specific low-rank weight matrices.

Result: VN demonstrates competitive performance across four benchmarks, matching baseline results in distribution and significantly reducing out-of-distribution error when faced with novel combinations of familiar factors.

Conclusion: The Vector Network architecture enables compositional generalization as an intrinsic feature of both architecture and inference, moving beyond the limitations of traditional dense parameter approaches.

Abstract: Deep networks are powerful function approximators, but they typically store many different computations in shared weight matrices, making it difficult to selectively reuse or adapt parts of them when a familiar structure appears in novel combinations. We introduce the Vector Network (VN), a hierarchical recurrent architecture in which each layer replaces a fixed weight matrix with a library of reusable rank-1 weight atoms. For each input, VN minimizes a layer-local energy to infer a sparse set of active weight atoms and their coefficients, jointly constrained by bottom-up input reconstruction and top-down feedback consistency. These weight atom coefficients then compose an input-specific low-rank weight matrix for that sample. After convergence, slow learning updates only the selected weight atoms through local residual signals scaled by the inferred coefficients. We evaluate VN on four compositional benchmarks spanning 1D signals, 2D spatial decoding, N-body dynamics, and compositional MNIST. VN matches strong baselines in distribution while often achieving out-of-distribution error about an order of magnitude lower when familiar factors must be recombined in novel ways. Vector networks thus make compositional generalization a structural property of the architecture and inference process rather than a brittle byproduct of fitting many behaviors into one shared dense parameter substrate.

</details>


### [428] [AOE: Exhaustive Out-of-Distribution Detection via Recalibrating Outlier Labels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28021)
*Fengqiang Wan,Qing-Yuan Jiang,Yang Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Adaptive Confidence Outlier Exposure (AOE), a method for enhancing out-of-distribution (OOD) detection by recalibrating outlier labels using temperature scaling, overcoming the limitations of uniform labeling in existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improving OOD detection to ensure reliability in machine learning systems when faced with novel inputs that differ from training data.

Method: AOE employs temperature scaling to generate adaptive soft targets from model predictions for OOD samples, preserving semantic relations while optimizing for a high-entropy distribution.

Result: Extensive experiments show that AOE significantly improves OOD detection performance compared to existing OE methods that utilize uniform labels.

Conclusion: By leveraging adaptive labels, AOE effectively enhances the separation margin between ID and OOD samples, mitigating overconfident predictions.

Abstract: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection is essential for deploying machine learning models in open-world and safety-critical scenarios, where test inputs may deviate from the training distribution and overconfident predictions on unknown samples can lead to unreliable decisions. Outlier Exposure (OE) has emerged as a promising OOD detection paradigm by introducing auxiliary outliers during training to enlarge the margin between in-distribution (ID) and OOD samples. Existing OE-based methods typically enlarge this margin by employing uniform labels to maximize the entropy of OOD samples over ID categories. However, we theoretically show that uniform labels inevitably disregard the relations between OOD samples and ID categories, termed the over-softening effect, leading to a suboptimal margin bound. Our theoretical analysis further reveals that explicitly exploiting such relations can instead yield improved OOD detection performance. Motivated by this insight, we propose \underline{A}daptive Confidence \underline{OE} (AOE), a simple yet effective method that leverages temperature scaling to recalibrate outlier labels. Specifically, AOE generates adaptive soft targets from temperature-scaled model predictions for OOD samples, where the learnable temperature smooths the prediction distribution without fully erasing class-wise relational information. By supervising OOD samples with these adaptive soft targets, AOE preserves the semantic proximity between OOD samples and ID categories while encouraging the softened targets to approach a high-entropy distribution, thereby suppressing overconfident OOD predictions and enlarging the separation margin. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of AOE.

</details>


### [429] [RW-TTT: Batched Serving for Request-Owned Test-Time Training State](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28053)
*Jian Yang,Zhizhuo Kou,Yao Tian,Hao Zhang,Han Chen,Sirui Han,Yike Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RW-TTT optimizes test-time training for LLMs, allowing efficient batching of requests with owner-tagged state updates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the inefficiencies and potential state corruption in test-time training (TTT) when applying adaptation techniques to large language models (LLMs) during generation.

Method: RW-TTT introduces tagged decode steps to manage request-owned states, batching only compatible phases and ensuring updates are committed only to their respective owners.

Result: Achieving an aggregate throughput of 274.61 tok/s on a single GPU, RW-TTT offers significant speed improvements over traditional sequential serving and per-stream replicas under the same memory constraints.

Conclusion: RW-TTT preserves LLM behavior on benchmarks while optimizing efficiency and preventing request state issues.

Abstract: Test-time training (TTT) adapts an LLM during generation by reading and updating request-owned state, such as fast weights, low-rank deltas, or streaming learner state. This breaks batched LLM serving, which assumes shared static weights: serial execution is correct but slow, while naive batching can corrupt request state. We formulate this problem as read-write TTT serving and present RW-TTT , which tags each decode step with its owner, version, and READ/WRITE effect, batches only compatible phases, and commits updates only to the owner. On one GPU with eight fast-weight InPlace-TTT streams, RW-TTT reaches 274.61 aggregate tok/s, 9.31x over sequential serving and 3.44x over per-stream replicas under the same memory budget. It preserves behavior on RULER, a long-context benchmark, and passes owner/version checks.

</details>


### [430] [On the Learnability of Test-Time Adaptation: A Recovery Complexity Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28057)
*Zhi Zhou,Ming Yang,Shi-Yu Tian,Kun-Yang Yu,Lan-Zhe Guo,Yu-Feng Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a theoretical framework for understanding test-time adaptation (TTA) in non-stationary environments, focusing on learnability, recovery complexity, and the trade-off between adaptability and information constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish a theoretical basis for TTA under non-stationary conditions, addressing the lack of existing frameworks that handle evolving distribution shifts and information constraints.

Method: Introduced the concepts of $(ε,δ)$-Recovery Complexity and $(ε,ρ)$-TTA Learnability, along with a novel discrete surrogate for non-stationary test streams to analyze gradual and abrupt shifts.

Result: Derivation of upper and lower bounds on recovery complexity, demonstrating fundamental limits of TTA and an intrinsic adaptivity-information trade-off.

Conclusion: Provided unified learnability guarantees for TTA, enhancing understanding beyond regret-based analyses.

Abstract: Test-time adaptation (TTA) aims to adapt models to maintain reliable performance on non-stationary test streams without requiring labeled data. Despite its empirical success, the learnability of TTA under non-stationary streams remains unexplored. A key challenge is the lack of a principled theoretical framework that simultaneously aligns with the TTA objective and captures both continuously evolving distribution shifts and intrinsic information constraints. To address this gap, we propose the first theoretical framework for studying the learnability of TTA and introduce $(ε,δ)$-Recovery Complexity and $(ε,ρ)$-TTA Learnability. Recovery complexity measures the post-shift time needed to maintain excess risk below a target level with high probability, and is further extended to TTA learnability, which measures the long-term reliability of TTA. Within this framework, we introduce a novel discrete surrogate for non-stationary test streams, enabling a unified and tractable analysis of both gradual and abrupt shifts. We derive order-wise matching lower and upper bounds on recovery complexity, revealing fundamental limits of TTA and an intrinsic adaptivity-information trade-off. These results provide unified learnability guarantees for TTA that complement regret-based analyses.

</details>


### [431] [PINE: Pruning Boosted Tree Ensembles with Conformal In-Distribution Prediction Equivalence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28068)
*Haruki Yajima,Yusuke Matsui*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PINE is a new pruning method for tree ensembles that balances compression and prediction fidelity, enhancing compression ratios without sacrificing decision consistency in machine learning models for tabular data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of standard pruning methods that risk altering predictions and compromising decision consistency, while also improving compression ratios of tree ensembles.

Method: PINE employs conformal calibration to manage the size of the in-distribution region and ensures preservation of prediction equivalence within that region through a single parameter $α$.

Result: PINE enhances the compression ratio by up to 30% across 12 public datasets, while maintaining prediction fidelity comparable to existing faithful pruning techniques.

Conclusion: PINE represents a significant advancement in pruning methods for tree ensembles, enabling better compression without sacrificing predictive performance.

Abstract: Tree ensembles are machine learning models with strong predictive performance and interpretability, and remain widely used for tabular data. Standard pruning methods for tree ensembles typically optimize an accuracy-compression trade-off and may change a subset of predictions, potentially compromising decision consistency. Faithful pruning methods address this issue by preserving prediction equivalence over the entire input space, but this requirement leads to lower compression ratios. We propose PINE, a pruning method that provides strong guarantees within an in-distribution region. PINE preserves prediction equivalence within this region and controls the region size using a single parameter $α$ via conformal calibration. Experiments on 12 public tabular datasets show that PINE improves the compression ratio by up to $30\%$ while preserving predictions at a comparable level to existing faithful pruning methods.

</details>


### [432] [Measure-to-measure Regression with Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28075)
*Matthew Vandergrift,Martha White,Yury Polyanskiy,Philippe Rigollet,Lazar Atanackovic*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a novel approach to measure-to-measure regression, addressing the limitations of existing methods by leveraging transformers for learning nonlinear transformations of probability measures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of predicting population evolution through unknown transformations, particularly in contexts like biology where collections of data points interact, necessitating a method to treat entire distributions as data.

Method: The authors formalize nonlinear M2M regression and propose two approaches: using transformers for static M2M maps and as dynamic M2M velocity fields, exploiting their mean-field structure.

Result: The proposed methods demonstrate effective generalization to unseen measures across various scenarios, including synthetic data, interacting particle systems, and real-world biological datasets.

Conclusion: This work advances the field of M2M regression by providing scalable and expressive methodologies that improve prediction accuracy in complex scientific applications.

Abstract: Many learning problems require predicting how populations evolve under an unknown transformation. A natural representation for such populations is a probability measure, with point clouds as a key example. In this work, we study the measure-to-measure (M2M) regression problem, in which one seeks to learn a map between probability measures from a finite collection of observed input-output pairs. In contrast to classical regression, where individual samples are transformed independently, M2M regression treats entire distributions as the data points. This perspective is vital in certain scientific applications, for example, cellular and molecular biology, where cells are known to evolve not as independent data points but as a collection. However, few existing approaches address the problem of M2M regression with sufficient expressivity and scalability. We present a formalization of nonlinear M2M regression and introduce two easy-to-use, expressive, and scalable approaches to learn such operators: transformers as static M2M maps and transformers as dynamic M2M velocity fields. Our approach leverages the natural measure-dependent and mean-field structure of transformers to learn nonlinear M2M maps on the space of probability distributions. We illustrate the effectiveness of our proposed method to generalize to unseen measures on synthetic experiments, interacting particle systems, and a large-scale patient-derived organoid dataset for predicting treatment response in colorectal cancer.

</details>


### [433] [Long Live The Balance: Information Bottleneck Driven Tree-based Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28109)
*Hao Jiang,Shurui Li,Tianpeng Bu,Bowen Xu,Xin Liu,Qihua Chen,Hongtao Duan,Lulu Hu,Bin Yang,Minying Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces IB-Score, a new metric for assessing exploration-exploitation balance in online RL for LLMs, and proposes IB-TPO, a framework that improves performance and efficiency in training with better balance in reasoning diversity and information sharing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance online reinforcement learning methods for large language models by addressing the imbalanced exploration-exploitation trade-off that leads to unstable optimization and suboptimal performance.

Method: Introducing a metric called IB-Score based on Information Bottleneck theory and developing a framework, Information Bottleneck-driven Tree-based Policy Optimization (IB-TPO), that optimizes this metric and incorporates a novel tree sampling strategy.

Result: IB-TPO significantly outperforms the GRPO baseline by 2.9% to 3.6% and outperforms other state-of-the-art online RL methods while also improving sampling efficiency by utilizing a tree structure for IB-Score estimation.

Conclusion: The proposed IB-TPO framework enhances the balance of exploration-exploitation in online RL for LLMs, leading to improved performance on standard benchmarks and more efficient online sampling.

Abstract: Recent advances in online reinforcement learning (RL) for large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated promising performance in complex reasoning tasks. However, they often exhibit an imbalanced exploration-exploitation trade-off, resulting in unstable optimization and sub-optimal performance. We introduce IB-Score, a novel metric grounded in Information Bottleneck theory that evaluates policy's exploration-exploitation balance by quantifying the trade-off between step-level reasoning diversity and mutual information shared with the correct answer. Analysis based on IB-Score shows that popular online RL approaches (e.g., GRPO) with common regularizers fail to consistently maintain balance during training with suboptimal results. To address this, we propose Information Bottleneck-driven Tree-based Policy Optimization (IB-TPO), a principled framework that formulates IB-Score as a fine-grained optimization objective and utilizes a novel IB-guided tree sampling strategy that not only improves the efficiency of online sampling with 50% more trajectories under the same token budget, but also reuses the tree structure for effective IB-Score Monte Carlo estimation. Extensive experiments across standard benchmarks show that our method significantly outperforms GRPO baseline by 2.9% to 3.6% and also outperforms other state-of-the-art online RL approaches. Our code is available at https://github.com/alibaba/EfficientRL.

</details>


### [434] [Chreode: A Cell World Model for One-Step Temporal Dynamics and Perturbation Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28111)
*Mufan Qiu,Genhui Zheng,Yinuo Xu,Ruichen Zhang,Ying Ding,Qi Long,Tianlong Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Chreode is a new model for predicting cell-state transitions under developmental signals and genetic changes. It innovatively handles data by training instead of inference and demonstrates notable accuracy improvements in cell differentiation predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The goal is to enhance predictions of transcriptional changes in cells during development and genetic perturbations, addressing limitations of existing models that either treat data statically or independently.

Method: Chreode employs a one-step cell model using a structured residual transition operator, allowing for efficient and rapid generation of cell-state transitions while dividing the process into distinct dynamics components.

Result: Chreode shows improved Sinkhorn distances in hematopoiesis and islet differentiation tasks, and significantly reduces error in gene-state embedding computations compared to existing models.

Conclusion: The results suggest that Chreode's pretrained dynamics can effectively predict perturbations and capture essential differentiation features, making it a robust tool for understanding cellular state transitions.

Abstract: Predicting how a cell will change its transcriptional state under a developmental signal or a genetic perturbation is the computational core of in-silico biology and the AI Virtual Cell program. Existing approaches either fit static control-to-treated maps that discard time, or solve multi-step ODE / Schrödinger-bridge problems on each dataset independently. We introduce Chreode, a one-step cell world model that predicts action-conditioned cell-state transitions through a structured residual transition operator. It shifts distributional evolution from inference time to training time, enabling single-pass generation while preserving a Waddington-inspired decomposition into downhill landscape flow, rotational in-tangent dynamics, and stochastic spread. The model is pretrained with a shared scVI encoder and a DiT-based dynamics backbone on a 2.4M-cell mouse embryonic atlas spanning 7 datasets. As a fine-tuning initialization, Chreode improves per-target Sinkhorn distance on Weinreb hematopoiesis and Veres islet differentiation over matched scratch models, PI-SDE, and PRESCIENT. As a transferable gene-state embedding for GEARS, the pretrained dynamics representation reduces shared-vocabulary DE20 mean squared error on Norman Perturb-seq from 0.2121 to 0.1858, a 12.4% relative improvement, without changing the GEARS training procedure. We interpret this transfer to perturbation prediction as evidence that pretrained developmental-trajectory dynamics encode differentiation primitives transferable to CRISPR-induced state shifts, since both involve cell-state transitions in a shared latent geometry. The pretrained backbone additionally produces zero-shot clonal fate scores on Weinreb that are competitive with strong dynamic-OT baselines.

</details>


### [435] [Adaptive Coarse-to-Fine Subgoal Refinement for Long-Horizon Offline Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28127)
*Kaiqiang Ke,Shenghong He,Chengdong Xu,Yuheng Luo,Xiangyuan Lan,Chao Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CFHRL introduces an adaptive framework for offline goal-conditioned reinforcement learning that refines distant goals into intermediate subgoals to improve performance on long-horizon tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon tasks in GCRL face challenges due to weak supervision from distant goal pairs and bootstrapping errors. Hierarchical methods provide some solutions, but sometimes fall short due to fixed abstractions.

Method: CFHRL adapts the goal refinement process by proposing intermediate targets starting from the final goal and stopping when a locally executable target is identified based on learned reachability costs.

Result: CFHRL demonstrates significant improvements in performance for various long-horizon tasks on OGBench, supported by experiments and ablations that validate its mechanisms.

Conclusion: The approach effectively enhances GCRL by allowing flexible goal refinement, thus making it more robust against challenges posed by distant state-goal reachability.

Abstract: Offline goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (GCRL) is challenging in long-horizon tasks, where distant state--goal pairs provide weak supervision and value estimates become vulnerable to accumulated bootstrapping errors. Hierarchical methods mitigate this difficulty by introducing intermediate subgoals, but fixed temporal abstractions or fixed hierarchy depths can be mismatched to state--goal pairs with different reachability horizons. We propose Coarse-to-Fine Hierarchical Goal Reinforcement Learning (CFHRL), a fully offline GCRL framework that adaptively refines distant goals before execution. Starting from the final goal, CFHRL recursively proposes intermediate targets, trained from replay-supported candidates, and stops refinement once the current target is estimated to be locally executable by a learned reachability cost. The key idea is that a subgoal need not be an exact midpoint or globally optimal waypoint; it only needs to provide reliable progress and reduce the remaining reaching difficulty, enabling subsequent refinement over shorter horizons. A stylized analysis further supports the robustness of approximate recursive contraction. Experiments on OGBench show substantial gains on several long-horizon tasks, with ablations validating the proposed refinement and stopping mechanisms

</details>


### [436] [Learning to Bid in Repeated Second-Price Auctions with Dynamic Values and Aggregated Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28133)
*Benjamin Heymann,Otmane Sakhi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper investigates dynamic bidding strategies in second-price auctions, where bidder values change based on past outcomes. It derives regret bounds for learning methods that optimize bidding, showing effective strategies that minimize regret as supported by numerical experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of dynamic bidder value in repeated second-price auctions and optimize bidding strategies accordingly.

Method: Analysis involves deriving regret bounds for learning methods combining plug-in estimators with an optimal policy characterized by differential equations.

Result: A specific confidence bound algorithm achieves near-optimal regret of $	ilde{O}(	ext{log} N)$ for piecewise linear primitives and $	ilde{O}(N^{1/3})$ for general, smooth primitives without explicit randomization.

Conclusion: The findings suggest effective learning strategies for dynamic bidding scenarios, validated through theoretical analysis and numerical experiments.

Abstract: We study the problem of learning to bid when the bidder's value is dynamic, i.e., when the current value depends on past outcomes. Specifically, we consider a bidder participating in repeated second-price auctions whose value depends on the time elapsed since their last successful bid, with auctions arriving in continuous time and only aggregated feedback revealed at the end of the horizon. Such a bidder must (1) balance the immediate benefit of winning the current auction against its impact on future values and (2) learn unknown environmental parameters. We derive regret bounds for a class of learning methods that combine plug-in estimators with a differential-equation characterization of the optimal policy, and show that a specific confidence bound algorithm learns the optimal policy with a near optimal regret of $\widetilde{O}(\log N)$ for piecewise linear primitives, and $\widetilde{O}(N^{1/3})$ for general, smooth primitives, achieving these regrets without explicit randomization. These theoretical results are supported by numerical experiments.

</details>


### [437] [Self-Consistency via Marginal Sharpening](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28142)
*Aleksei Arzhantsev,Otmane Sakhi,Nicolas Chopin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a novel inference-time sampling method that enhances reasoning in language models by focusing on answer distribution rather than full-output sequences, yielding faster and more effective results in mathematics and coding tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve logical reasoning in language models during inference without requiring additional training by shifting focus from full-output distribution to answer marginals.

Method: A simple autoregressive parallel sampling algorithm is introduced that approximates the sharpened answer marginal, allowing for efficient sampling.

Result: The proposed method outperforms standard power sampling techniques on mathematics and coding benchmarks while being significantly faster.

Conclusion: Targeting the answer marginal improves reasoning abilities in language models during inference, leading to enhanced performance in specific tasks.

Abstract: Inference-time sampling can elicit strong reasoning abilities from language models without additional training. Existing power-sampling methods do so by sharpening the distribution over full generated outputs, favoring completions that are individually likely under the model. We argue that this is the wrong object to target for reasoning: a completion entangles a reasoning trace with a final answer, whereas what matters is whether an answer is supported by many plausible reasoning paths. We therefore shift the target from the full-output distribution to the sharpened answer marginal, making self-consistency an inference-time objective rather than a post-hoc voting criterion. Surprisingly, this marginal target admits an efficient approximation: we propose a simple, purely autoregressive parallel sampling algorithm that approximately samples from the sharpened answer marginal, eliciting stronger performance than standard power sampling on mathematics and coding benchmarks while being orders of magnitude faster.

</details>


### [438] [Sign-Aware Gated Sparse Autoencoders: Modeling Anticorrelated Features with Bi-Jump-ReLU Activations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28149)
*Bartosz Wieciech,Zmnako Awrahman,Marcin Czelej,Victor Hugo Jaramillo Velasquez,Wioletta Stobieniecka*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Sign-Aware Gated Sparse Autoencoder (SA-GSAE), addressing limitations of standard Sparse Autoencoders by allowing bipolar encoding of diametrically opposed concepts, optimizing feature extraction from Large Language Models (LLMs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional Sparse Autoencoders impose non-negativity constraints leading to inefficient feature representation by creating separate latents for conflicting concepts, thus reducing dictionary capacity.

Method: The SA-GSAE employs a polarity-sensitive gate for signed support selection, incorporates signed-magnitude to prevent L1 shrinkage, and adds auxiliary reconstruction to maintain gate functionality. A new Bi-Jump-ReLU activation is introduced to achieve bipolar feature sharing.

Result: The SA-GSAE shows improved performance over standard Gated SAEs, significantly reducing dead fraction and improving encoding efficiency, particularly in MLP-output cells, and effectively handling anticorrelated features.

Conclusion: The half-width SA-GSAE outperforms full-width alternatives in efficiency and reconstruction stability, indicating that bipolar encoding approaches are effective in feature representation within LLMs.

Abstract: Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) extract interpretable features from Large Language Models, but standard variants enforce non-negativity, forcing separate latents for diametrically opposed concepts (e.g., "pressure too high" vs. "pressure too low") and wasting dictionary capacity when features are anticorrelated. We propose the Sign-Aware Gated SAE (SA-GSAE): two-sided gated sparsity with signed magnitude and auxiliary supervision. A polarity-sensitive gate selects support on either sign, a signed-magnitude path avoids L1 shrinkage, and an auxiliary reconstruction prevents gate collapse. Bipolar sharing - one latent encoding both signs along a shared direction - is realised via a new Bi-Jump-ReLU activation; parameter accounting shows sign-awareness stays parameter-efficient even when anticorrelated pairs are rare.
  On real LLM activations across three mid-depth hookpoints on Pythia-1B and SmolLM3-3B (6 cells, 3 seeds), a half-width SA-GSAE at width H strictly Pareto-dominates a full-width Gated SAE at 2H over the entire swept L0 overlap on 3 of 6 cells (both MLP-output hookpoints and resid-mid/Pythia-1B); on the remaining 3 it matches R^2 within 0.025 (max gap -0.008) while cutting dead fraction by 0.35-0.62 absolute. Sweep-geomean dead-fraction reductions are ~100x-500x on MLP-output cells and Pythia-1B resid, ~2x-4x on attention cells and SmolLM3-3B resid. Ablations show the two-sided gate and auxiliary loss are load-bearing (no auxiliary collapses LR to 0.27, 98% dead); tying r_i^+ = r_i^- is indistinguishable (|Delta R^2| = 0.0015), and we recommend this symmetric variant as default. MLP-output gains come from most latents carrying both polarities; on attention, bipolar structure concentrates in a small set of top latents. Full-width SA-GSAE exhibits a reproducible reconstruction collapse at SmolLM3-3B resid that the half-width entirely avoids.

</details>


### [439] [Off-Policy Learning to Reason Works Because It Is More Pessimistic Than You Think](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28150)
*Otmane Sakhi,Aleksei Arzhantsev,Imad Aouali,Flavian Vasile*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper explores off-policy reinforcement learning in large language models, proposing a framework that optimizes toward conservative target policies to enhance stability and effectiveness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenges of lagged or asynchronous updates in large-scale reinforcement learning, which can destabilize training and increase variance due to reliance on off-policy data.

Method: The paper constructs off-policy objectives that rely on implicit pessimism, optimizing toward more conservative policies and improving stability without using importance weights.

Result: The proposed framework effectively stabilizes the induced distribution of target policies, leading to enhanced off-policy learning effectiveness compared to traditional methods that attempt to correct distribution mismatches.

Conclusion: By embracing off-policy data and focusing on conservative targets, the proposed method provides a more stable and effective approach to training large language models in reinforcement learning.

Abstract: Large scale reinforcement learning has become a central tool for improving reasoning in large language models. At this scale, generation is often lagged or asynchronous, so updates are performed on data collected by older policies. This makes learning inherently off-policy. Most existing approaches nevertheless remain rooted in PPO-style trust-region objectives, treating training as approximately on-policy and using importance weights to correct distribution mismatch. These corrections can introduce high variance, destabilize optimization, and accelerate entropy collapse. Recent work suggests an alternative: rather than correcting the mismatch, one can embrace off-policy data and remove importance weights, often yielding stronger algorithms. In this paper, we provide an intuitive construction of off-policy objectives that include successful off-policy objectives and show that their effectiveness can be understood through implicit pessimism: they optimize toward target policies that are more conservative than their nominal objectives suggest. This perspective explains why some particular implementation choices improve stability: they implicitly control the effective target distribution. We then propose a principled modification that stabilize this induced distribution and improve off-policy learning.

</details>


### [440] [Temporal Hyperbolic Graph Representation Learning for Scale-Free Internet Routing and Delay Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28155)
*Yi-Ling Kuo,Hao-Yu Tien,Shih-Yu Tsai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HERMIT, a hybrid framework for predicting Internet round-trip time (RTT), combines hyperbolic temporal graph neural networks with Random Forest regression, improving accuracy in latency prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predicting RTT is crucial for routing optimization and QoS but is challenging due to long-term dependencies and complex routing dynamics, which standard models struggle to handle.

Method: HERMIT integrates a hyperbolic manifold-preserving temporal GNN with a Random Forest regressor, using RTT-aware edge features and a learnable edge encoder for enhanced link state modeling.

Result: HERMIT outperforms a Random Forest baseline by achieving a 6% RMSE improvement and reducing errors on heavy-tailed latency samples, and also surpasses previous hyperbolic TGNN models.

Conclusion: The hybrid approach of combining hyperbolic temporal graph learning with Random Forest regression shows promise for scalable and effective RTT prediction in real-world Internet networks.

Abstract: Predicting Internet round-trip time (RTT) is critical for routing optimization, quality-of-service (QoS) provisioning, and traffic engineering, yet remains challenging due to long-term temporal dependencies, evolving routing dynamics, and heavy-tailed latency distributions. While Temporal Graph Neural Networks (TGNNs) can model evolving network topologies, most existing approaches operate in Euclidean space, which poorly captures the hierarchical and scale-free structure of Internet routing graphs. Hyperbolic geometry provides a more suitable representation space.
  We propose HERMIT (Hyperbolic Edge-aware RTT Modeling via Integrated Topology), a hybrid framework combining a hyperbolic manifold-preserving temporal GNN with a Random Forest regressor for joint link prediction and RTT prediction. Built on HMPTGN, HERMIT introduces RTT-aware edge features and a learnable edge encoder to improve modeling of evolving link states and routing behavior. The resulting hyperbolic node representations are combined with historical RTT statistics for robust latency prediction.
  We evaluate HERMIT on a large-scale real Internet dataset spanning 2015-2024. HERMIT consistently outperforms a strong Random Forest baseline using only historical RTT statistics, achieving a 6% RMSE improvement while reducing large errors on heavy-tailed samples. It also surpasses prior hyperbolic TGNN models, including HMPTGN and HTGN, in link prediction performance. These results demonstrate that combining hyperbolic temporal graph learning with tree-based regression provides a scalable solution for RTT prediction in real-world Internet topologies.

</details>


### [441] [Unification and Optimization of Robust Supervised Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28165)
*Jonas Hanselle,Valentin Margraf,Clemens Damke,Eyke Hüllermeier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a unified design space for robust learning methods, allowing for joint hyperparameter optimization across various failure modes in machine learning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations of isolated robust learning methods and provide a flexible solution for practitioners unclear on dominant failure modes.

Method: Organizing existing robust methods along three design axes and developing a training procedure that sequentially addresses reference distribution enrichment, input-space and label-space perturbations, and sample-level aggregation with three stance options.

Result: Joint hyperparameter optimization demonstrates competitive performance against the best single-method baseline across various benchmarks, including tabular data, images, and reinforcement learning.

Conclusion: The proposed framework offers a reliable default for practitioners, allowing them to effectively configure robustness strategies without prior knowledge of dominant failure modes.

Abstract: The literature has proposed various robust alternatives to empirical risk minimisation to address failure modes such as distribution shift, label noise and finite-sample degeneracies. Examples include distributionally robust optimization, label smoothing, vicinal risk minimization, and Mixup. However, such approaches are typically developed in isolation, forcing practitioners to commit a priori to a single failure mode even when the dominant mode for the task is unclear. To address this, we organize a broad class of existing methods along three common design axes and derive a tractable training procedure that decomposes robust learning into sequential stages (reference distribution enrichment, input-space perturbation, label-space perturbation, and sample-level aggregation), each with a choice of stance (pessimistic, neutral, or optimistic). This results in a unified design space in which joint hyperparameter optimization can compose and configure robustness strategies suited to the task at hand. Across tabular, image, and reward modeling benchmarks, joint hyperparameter optimization is competitive with the best single-method baseline in each setting, offering a reliable default for practitioners who do not know a priori which failure mode dominates their task.

</details>


### [442] [QuITE: Query-Based Irregular Time Series Embedding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28166)
*JungHoon Lim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces QuITE, a novel embedding module for irregular multivariate time series (IMTS) that enhances modeling by using learnable query tokens to aggregate observations, avoiding distortion from interpolation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current modeling techniques for irregular multivariate time series are limited by either specialized architectures or interpolation methods that can distort data, indicating a need for a more effective embedding solution.

Method: QuITE is introduced as a plug-and-play embedding module utilizing learnable query tokens with a self-attention mechanism, facilitating direct integration into existing multivariate time series models without modifying their architecture.

Result: Experiments demonstrate that QuITE significantly enhances forecasting and classification accuracy for MTS models, achieving average relative improvements of up to 54.7% in forecasting and 15.8% in classification metrics across various datasets.

Conclusion: QuITE effectively addresses the inherent challenges of IMTS modeling by producing more accurate latent representations, thereby allowing for significant performance improvements in standard MTS frameworks.

Abstract: Irregular Multivariate Time Series (IMTS) are common in practice, yet their irregular sampling complicates effective modeling. Existing approaches typically either (i) design specialized architectures that limit the reuse of proven Multivariate Time Series (MTS) models, or (ii) map IMTS onto regular temporal grids through interpolation, which may distort temporal dynamics by introducing artificial values. To address these limitations, we propose a new input-embedding-based approach. We identify that the key bottleneck lies not in the backbone architecture, but in conventional embedding layers that assume uniform sampling. In this work, we introduce QuITE (Query-Based Irregular Time Series Embedding), a simple yet effective plug-and-play embedding module for IMTS. QuITE employs learnable query tokens to aggregate irregular observations through a single self-attention layer, directly producing backbone-compatible latent representations without artificial value generation or architectural modification. Extensive experiments on real-world benchmarks show that QuITE consistently improves MTS models, yielding average relative gains of up to $54.7\%$ in forecasting and $15.8\%$ in classification across diverse datasets and backbone architectures. Code is available at: https://github.com/Meaningfull9502/QuITE.

</details>


### [443] [Joint Training of Multi-Token Prediction in Reinforcement Learning via Optimal Coefficient Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28184)
*Zili Wang,Jiajun Chai,Lin Chen,Xiaohan Wang,Shiming Xiang,Guojun Yin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates the integration of Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) with reinforcement learning (RL) for large language models, addressing performance issues in joint training and proposing Optimal Coefficient Calibration (OCC) to enhance training efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The goal is to improve the reasoning capabilities of large language models by effectively combining Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) with Multi-Token Prediction (MTP).

Method: The authors analyze the optimization dynamics of MTP in RL training, decomposing the effect of MTP on the RL objective into a first-order correlation and a second-order perturbation penalty, then propose an adaptive scheme called Optimal Coefficient Calibration (OCC).

Result: OCC tracks the optimal coefficient online and is shown to match or exceed the performance of the detach baseline across six competitive benchmarks in mathematical reasoning.

Conclusion: The study concludes that OCC enhances the performance of joint MTP-RL training by effectively balancing the contributions of MTP in the optimization process.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as the standard paradigm for improving reasoning capability of large language models, while Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) has been a widely adopted module in pretraining. Combining them is a natural approach, yet current RL practices detach MTP gradients because joint training degrades the performance. We revisit this failure from an optimization perspective. We show that the per-step effect of MTP on the RL objective can be decomposed into two terms: a first-order correlation and a second-order perturbation penalty. This decomposition unifies three MTP training regimes: Detach, Cross-Entropy loss, and Policy loss, and explains why each succeeds or fails. Further analysis of policy loss reveals that, although it aligns with intuition, performance still degrades: the correlation term decays while the quadratic penalty persists. Guided by the analysis, we propose Optimal Coefficient Calibration (OCC), an adaptive scheme that tracks the optimal coefficient online via a log-probability proxy at negligible cost. Across six competition-level mathematical reasoning benchmarks, OCC consistently matches or exceeds the detach baseline, delivering improved joint MTP-RL training performance.

</details>


### [444] [Hierarchical Synthetic Tabular Data Generation: A Hybrid Top-Down and Bottom-Up Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28198)
*Junfeng Nie,Alvin Jin,Xiaohui Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes H-TDBU framework for synthetic tabular data generation combining top-down logic and bottom-up statistical learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods in synthetic data generation are limited by issues like data heterogeneity and robustness, especially in low-data scenarios.

Method: The H-TDBU framework employs a dual approach: top-down creates logical constraints and alignment, while bottom-up learns local patterns from real data using tabular generators, combined in a unified synthesis engine.

Result: The framework demonstrates improved performance in generating synthetic data on multimodal financial benchmarks, outperforming neural baseline methods.

Conclusion: The use of hierarchical rule-guided synthesis effectively enhances control, coherence, and fidelity in generating synthetic data.

Abstract: Existing approaches for synthetic tabular data generation are based on either purely generative models or LLMs, both of which struggle with data heterogeneity, logical consistency, rare-event coverage, and robustness in low-data regimes. In this paper, we propose a hierarchical hybrid top-down and bottom-up (H-TDBU) framework that decouples semantic structures from stochastic texture. In the top-down path, structure-driven logical constraints and cross-modal alignment rules are constructed, while in the bottom-up path, lightweight tabular generators are used to learn local statistical patterns from real data. The two paths are consolidated in a unified synthesis engine with an iterative feedback loop. We evaluate the framework on weak multimodal financial benchmarks combining tabular and sentiment-text data. Experimental results show that our H-TDBU approach improves train-synthetic-test-real performance over neural baseline methods while preserving semantic consistency. Our results suggest that hierarchical rule-guided synthesis provides an effective mechanism for combining controllability, semantic coherence, and statistical fidelity in synthetic data generation.

</details>


### [445] [Geometry-First Generative Spatial Single-Cell Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28200)
*Ehtesamul Azim,Muhtasim Noor Alif,Tae Hyun Hwang,Yanjie Fu,Wei Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GEARS is a geometry-first framework that reconstructs single-cell spatial geometry from spatial transcriptomics without relying on traditional methods or labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for integrating scRNA-seq and ST either lose spatial context or depend on fixed coordinate systems, which can be inadequate in unpaired data settings.

Method: GEARS employs a domain-invariant expression encoder to align ST spots and scRNA-seq cells and uses a permutation-equivariant generator alongside a diffusion-based refiner to reconstruct spatial geometries.

Result: GEARS improves global distance preservation, local neighborhood fidelity, and spatial alignment in comparisons with existing spatial mapping and deconvolution methods.

Conclusion: The extensive evaluations demonstrate the efficiency of GEARS in accurately reconstructing spatial geometries from single-cell data.

Abstract: Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) profiles large numbers of cells but loses spatial context, whereas spatial transcriptomics (ST) preserves partial spatial structure at lower resolution. Most existing integration methods either deconvolve spot mixtures or map cells onto a measured spot lattice, which ties reconstructions to a fixed grid and slide-specific coordinate systems, a limitation that is especially problematic in unpaired settings. We propose GEARS, a geometry-first framework that reconstructs an intrinsic single-cell spatial geometry guided by ST, without relying on cell-type labels, histological images, or cell-to-spot assignment. GEARS first learns a domain-invariant expression encoder that aligns ST spots and dissociated cells, and then trains a permutation-equivariant generator with a diffusion-based refiner with EDM-style preconditioning to generate local spatial geometries under pose-invariant supervision derived from ST coordinates. At inference, GEARS reconstructs geometry on many overlapping subsets of scRNA-seq cells, aggregates predicted pairwise distances across subsets, and solves a global distance-geometry problem to obtain canonical two-dimensional coordinates and a dense distance matrix. Extensive quantitative and qualitative experiments, including cross-section generalization, show that GEARS consistently improves global distance preservation, local neighborhood fidelity, and spatial distribution alignment compared to strong spatial mapping and deconvolution baselines.

</details>


### [446] [Robust Contrastive Graph Clustering with Adaptive Local-Global Integration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28209)
*Lei Zhang,Fubo Sun,Haipeng Yang,Zhong Guan,Likang Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel contrastive graph clustering framework that integrates multi-scale local structures with global semantics using attention mechanisms, addressing limitations in existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to enhance graph clustering by improving the representation of nodes in complex graphs that exhibit fragmented structures and ambiguous cluster boundaries.

Method: The proposed method uses GNN-based topological signals and attention mechanisms to fuse local structures at multiple depths and aggregate global semantics from evolving cluster centers, trained through a dual-view contrastive learning approach.

Result: The model demonstrates improved clustering performance on eight real-world graph datasets compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that the proposed framework effectively enhances node representations in graph clustering tasks by leveraging both local and global structural information.

Abstract: Graph clustering is essential in graph analysis for revealing structural patterns and node communities. Despite recent advances in self-supervised contrastive learning that have improved clustering via structural and attribute signals, existing methods still struggle to flexibly capture high-order local structures and often overlook global semantics in complex graphs. These limitations lead to suboptimal node representations, especially in real-world graphs with fragmented structures and ambiguous cluster boundaries. To address these limitations, a contrastive graph clustering framework is proposed to jointly integrate multi-scale local structures with global semantics via attention mechanisms. At the local level, GNN-based topological signals extracted from multiple propagation depths are adaptively fused through attention-based weighting to capture multi-scale neighborhood features. At the global level, semantic prototypes derived from dynamically evolving cluster centers are adaptively aggregated through attention to guide node representations and enhance inter-cluster separability. The model is trained under a dual-view contrastive learning paradigm with a hybrid objective that combines instance-level and structure-aware losses to improve representation robustness and discrimination. Experiments on eight real-world graph datasets demonstrate that our method achieves competitive clustering performance. Code is available at https://github.com/vege12138/w2.

</details>


### [447] [PhAME: Phenotype-Aware Molecular Editing via Latent Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28226)
*Łukasz Janisiów,Sebastian Musiał,Bartosz Zieliński,Dawid Rymarczyk,Tomasz Danel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PhAME is a novel framework for optimizing small-molecule drug candidates by addressing the limitations of existing generative methods, enabling precise molecular editing based on biological signatures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve small-molecule drug discovery by optimizing multiple properties of drug candidates while ensuring phenotypic accuracy and structural similarity to known compounds.

Method: PhAME employs a latent diffusion framework and a compositional classifier-free guidance scheme, operating in the latent space of a pretrained graph-based variational autoencoder (VAE) to facilitate molecular editing.

Result: PhAME outperforms existing generative methods in empirical evaluations across various benchmarks, showing enhanced performance in docking score optimization and multimodal phenotypic generation.

Conclusion: The framework provides a powerful tool for molecular optimization, achieving a balance between desired biological properties and chemical validity, helping advance drug discovery efforts.

Abstract: Small-molecule drug discovery requires simultaneous optimization of numerous properties of candidate molecules. These properties can be investigated through the analysis of high-dimensional biological signatures, such as cell morphology and transcriptomic perturbations, which provide a rich perspective on the underlying biological mechanisms. However, existing generative methods, which use those signatures for optimization, fail to meet two key requirements: providing precise guidance toward desired phenotypic signatures while maintaining structural proximity to a known hit. We introduce PhAME (Phenotype-Aware Molecular Editing), a latent diffusion framework that overcomes this challenge by recasting molecular optimization as editing in the latent space of a pretrained graph-based VAE. Our central contribution is a compositional classifier-free guidance scheme with two independent scales, one for the phenotype-conditioning and one for similarity to the seed structure, allowing practitioners to control the tradeoff between these two objectives. Empirical evaluations across diverse benchmarks, including docking score optimization and multimodal phenotypic generation, demonstrate that PhAME achieves state-of-the-art results while maintaining high chemical validity and novelty.

</details>


### [448] [Refining Multidimensional Video Reward Models via Disentangled Influence Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28203)
*Muyao Wang,Zeke Xie,Hideki Nakayama*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper addresses challenges in training Multidimensional Video Reward Models (MVRMs) for Text-to-Video generation due to variable reliability across evaluation dimensions. It introduces a disentangled influence framework to assess dimension-specific supervision risks and proposes two strategies for refinement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The complexity of video evaluation in T2V models necessitates a nuanced assessment that reflects human visual perception, which is hindered by the inconsistent reliability of training samples across different dimensions.

Method: The authors propose a disentangled influence framework to estimate supervision risk specific to each evaluation dimension, along with two strategies: Dimension-Disentangled Pruning and Dimension-Disentangled Reweighting.

Result: The proposed strategies significantly improve the performance of reward models, providing better alignment with ground truth compared to traditional global filtering methods.

Conclusion: The paper highlights the importance of addressing dimensional heterogeneity in T2V training, paving the way for more effective evaluation models.

Abstract: As Text-to-Video (T2V) generation models continue to evolve, the complexity of video evaluation necessitates a fine-grained assessment across various axes. To address this, recent works have focused on developing Multidimensional Video Reward Models (MVRMs), which decompose the evaluation process to better align with the multifaceted nature of human visual perception. However, training effective MVRMs is fundamentally challenged by the complex nature of video data. In this work, we identify a critical phenomenon termed Dimensional Heterogeneity: the reliability of a training sample can vary substantially across evaluation dimensions, meaning that a sample may provide reliable supervision for one objective while inducing high supervision risk for another. Consequently, prevailing data-centric methods that filter based on global scalar metrics are ill-posed for T2V tasks. To address this, we propose a disentangled influence framework that that efficiently estimates dimension-specific supervision risk. Leveraging this framework, we introduce two dimension-disentangled refinement strategies: Dimension-Disentangled Pruning, which removes extreme high-risk samples, and Dimension-Disentangled Reweighting, which softly down-weights high-risk supervision. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our disentangled strategies significantly outperform global filtering baselines, yielding reward models with superior alignment to ground truth.

</details>


### [449] [IRDS: Interpretable RLVR Data Selection via Verifier-Coupled Sparse Autoencoder Coverage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28247)
*Yuhan Li,Mingxu Zhang,Dazhong Shen,Ying Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces IRDS, a method for improving data efficiency in RLVR training for LLMs by selecting instances that enhance learning while being interpretable and auditable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improving data efficiency in reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards due to its critical role in enhancing LLM reasoning and the limitations of existing methods.

Method: IRDS utilizes a sparse autoencoder to select RLVR training instances based on a verifier-coupled coverage objective, employing greedy log-determinant maximization for selection.

Result: IRDS outperforms the strongest baseline in accuracy on multiple benchmarks, with improvements of +3.9/+4.0 pp for Qwen models and +0.5 pp for Llama-3.1-8B, all while being significantly cheaper than previous methods.

Conclusion: IRDS not only enhances data efficiency and accuracy in RLVR training but also provides an interpretable and auditable selection process.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become a key technique for en- hancing LLM reasoning, yet its data ineffi- ciency remains a major bottleneck. Existing methods address this problem only partially, each missing at least one of subset-level cov- erage, verifier signal use, or interpretability. To address this gap, we present IRDS (Inter- pretable RLVR Data Selection), which selects RLVR training instances on a sparse autoen- coder (SAE) cluster basis so the selection itself is auditable on recognizable problem motifs. To select instances the model both fails on and can still learn from, we introduce a verifier- coupled coverage objective on the SAE basis and solve it by greedy log-determinant max- imization. Experiments on three instruction- tuned models and six math reasoning bench- marks show that IRDS achieves the highest overall accuracy, exceeding the strongest base- line by +3.9/+4.0 pp on the two Qwen models and by +0.5 pp on Llama-3.1-8B, while run- ning an order of magnitude cheaper than the trajectory-based baseline.

</details>


### [450] [Parameter-Efficient Generative Modeling with Controlled Vector Fields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28267)
*Peyman Morteza*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents a novel generative modeling framework utilizing fixed vector fields and learned controls for efficient data transport, guided by the Chow-Rashevskii theorem.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a parameter-efficient generative model that leverages fixed vector fields and scalar control functions for expressive data transport, overcoming limitations of high-dimensional vector fields.

Method: The framework uses a combination of bracket-generating fixed vector fields modulated by learned scalar functions to define the transport velocity, addressing parameter efficiency and interpretability.

Result: The proposed model demonstrates effective transport capabilities for source to target distributions on synthetic datasets, adhering to expressivity principles under certain assumptions.

Conclusion: The research validates the potential of the framework for structured generative modeling, highlighting its efficiency and expressiveness compared to traditional methods.

Abstract: We introduce a continuous-time generative modeling framework, motivated by the Chow-Rashevskii theorem, that builds expressive flows from a small set of fixed vector fields and learned scalar controls. Instead of learning an unconstrained high-dimensional vector field, our framework constructs the velocity by modulating fixed vector fields with learned scalar control functions. When the fixed fields are bracket-generating, their Lie algebra spans the ambient space, providing a mechanism for expressive transport with only a small number of learned control channels and offering a parameter-efficient geometric alternative to standard vector-field parameterizations. This decoupled formulation yields a structured and interpretable generative model in which the number of learned scalar output channels can be chosen independently of the ambient dimension. We formulate an expressivity principle showing that, under suitable controllability and well-posedness assumptions, such controlled flows can transport a source distribution to a target distribution. We train the resulting model using a continuous-normalizing-flow likelihood objective and present proof-of-concept experiments on synthetic distributions.

</details>


### [451] [Dynamic Topic Modeling with a Higher-Order Hypergraphical Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28269)
*Hanjia Gao,Hanwen Ye,Qing Nie,Annie Qu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a novel hypergraph-based dynamic topic modeling framework that overcomes limitations of traditional models by effectively separating word occurrence from repetition and enhancing the analysis of evolving trends in diverse datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the limitations of traditional topic models that conflate word occurrence and repetition, leading to restricted dependence structure and overlooking higher-order interactions, especially in dynamic corpora.

Method: The authors propose a hypergraph representation where documents are hyperedges connecting co-occurring words, with repetition encoded as node weights. A hypergraph-based multinomial distribution is developed, and dynamic topic modeling is implemented using structured low-rank factorizations with temporal regularization.

Result: Experiments show that the proposed framework consistently outperforms existing multinomial-based topic models on synthetic data and in analyses of the ICLR corpus.

Conclusion: The framework enhances dynamic topic modeling by addressing limitations of existing approaches, providing local convergence guarantees and non-asymptotic error bounds, making it a robust tool for analyzing evolving topics.

Abstract: Dynamic topic modeling is widely used to analyze evolving trends in scientific literature, medical records, and social media. Traditional topic models represent each topic through a single probability vector on the multinomial simplex and implicitly couple word occurrence and repetition within one probabilistic mechanism. However, this formulation restricts the dependence structure among words and overlooks informative higher-order interactions, particularly in dynamic corpora with overlapping semantics. To address these limitations, we introduce a hypergraph representation of text where each document is modeled as a hyperedge connecting all co-occurring words, with repetition intensities encoded as node weights. This representation naturally separates word occurrence from repetition and induces a novel hypergraph-based multinomial distribution with a nonlinear normalization depending on the observed word set of each document. Building on this likelihood, we develop a dynamic topic modeling framework via structured low-rank factorizations with explicit temporal regularization on topic-word profiles. Moreover, we establish local convergence guarantees and derive non-asymptotic error bounds despite the intrinsic nonconvexity induced by bilinear factorization and document-specific nonlinear normalization. Numerical experiments on synthetic data and an application to the International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR) corpus demonstrate consistent improvements over existing multinomial-based topic models.

</details>


### [452] [Commit to the Bit: Reactive Reinforcement Learning Done Right](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28276)
*Onno Eberhard,Claire Vernade,Michael Muehlebach*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents Committed Q-learning, an algorithm that converges to an optimal reactive policy in non-Markovian environments with deterministic observations, under the new assumption of rewire-robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional reinforcement learning approaches assume a Markovian framework, which is often not applicable in real-world scenarios where environments are partially observable or involve function approximation.

Method: The proposed Committed Q-learning algorithm commits to a single action when entering a feature and resamples actions only when the feature changes, ensuring effective learning in quasi-Markov environments.

Result: The algorithm is proven to converge almost surely to the optimal reactive policy, which is a significant improvement over previous methods that relied on the $q_	au$-realizability condition.

Conclusion: Committed Q-learning offers a viable solution for learning optimal policies in environments that exhibit non-Markovian characteristics, expanding the applicability of reinforcement learning algorithms.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning algorithms are commonly analyzed (and designed) under the Markov assumption. This is unrealistic, as most environments encountered in practice are either partially observable, or require function approximation that restricts the agent to access non-Markovian state features. We consider the problem of learning an optimal reactive policy in a finite environment with deterministic observations (or equivalently, hard state aggregation). We introduce a new algorithm, Committed Q-learning, and prove almost-sure convergence to the optimal reactive policy under an intuitive assumption we call rewire-robustness. This assumption is strictly weaker than the $q_\star$-realizability condition used in prior work. Our algorithm is a variant of classical Q-learning in which the behavior policy commits to a single action upon entering a feature, and only resamples actions when the observed feature changes. A crucial part of our analysis is the introduction of quasi-Markov environments.

</details>


### [453] [AtomComposer: Discovering Chemical Space from First Principles with Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28287)
*Bjarke Hastrup,Francois Cornet,Tejs Vegge,Arghya Bhowmik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces AtomComposer, an autonomous agent for discovering stable molecules without training data, using online reinforcement learning to explore vast chemical spaces and construct valid 3D isomers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of existing molecular generative models that depend on pre-curated datasets, biases, and restricted exploration.

Method: AtomComposer employs a multi-composition training scheme and reinforcement learning to autonomously generate valid 3D isomers based on energy and validity rewards.

Result: AtomComposer discovers significantly more valid isomers than traditional single-composition reinforcement-learning models, showcasing its ability to generalize across diverse chemistry.

Conclusion: The findings support online reinforcement learning as an effective approach for scalable exploration in chemical configuration spaces.

Abstract: Discovering novel stable molecules without training data remains a grand scientific challenge. Current molecular generative models are trained on large, pre-curated datasets, which introduce biases and limit exploration of novel chemistry. In contrast, we propose a new paradigm: autonomous, generalized agents capable of mapping vast, unknown chemical spaces without any pretraining. For the first time, we present AtomComposer, a self-guided agent that autonomously constructs valid 3D isomers under stoichiometric constraints and is trained exclusively online using reinforcement learning. Unlike existing approaches that generally overfit to a specific chemical formula, we establish a multi-composition training scheme that enables a broad generalization across diverse chemistry, guided by energy- and validity-based rewards. Our agent can discover up to an order of magnitude more valid isomers on unseen test formulas than existing single-composition reinforcement-learning baselines trained with per-step energy rewards. These results fulfill the promise of online reinforcement learning as a powerful paradigm for scalable, from-scratch exploration of chemical configuration space.

</details>


### [454] [Adaptive Bandit Algorithms for Contextual Matching Markets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28290)
*Shiyun Lin,Simon Mauras,Vianney Perchet,Nadav Merlis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper explores bandit learning in matching markets, focusing on minimizing players' regret against stable benchmarks when new arms with observable contexts are introduced.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation of this study is to address the challenges of bandit learning in matching markets where players’ utilities are influenced by complex contextual factors that can lead to significant regret variations.

Method: The paper introduces a novel minimum preference gap for the stochastic case and proposes adaptive algorithms for both stochastic and adversarial contexts to achieve specified regret bounds.

Result: In the stochastic setting, the work establishes an instance-dependent poly-logarithmic regret upper bound and matching instance-independent bounds. In the adversarial setting, a sublinear regret bound is achieved using an adaptive algorithm.

Conclusion: The findings highlight the complexities of resource allocation in dynamic environments and provide robust solutions to achieve minimized regret in both stochastic and adversarial settings.

Abstract: We study bandit learning in matching markets, where players and arms constitute the two market sides, and the players' utilities are linear in the arm contexts. In each round, new arms arrive with observable contexts. Then, the algorithm matches them to players, aiming to minimize each player's regret against a stable matching benchmark. This contextual structure creates significant complexity: subtle context shifts can slightly alter one player's utility while completely reconfiguring the underlying benchmark, causing large regret spikes for others. We address this in two settings: stochastic contexts, drawn from a latent distribution, and adversarial contexts, which may be arbitrary. For the stochastic case, we introduce a novel minimum preference gap to capture learning difficulty and provide a fully adaptive algorithm with an instance-dependent poly-logarithmic regret upper bound. We also establish matching instance-independent regret upper and lower bounds under a mild distributional assumption. For the adversarial setting, we propose a tractable regret notion that remains valid under arbitrary contexts and achieves an instance-independent sublinear regret bound via an adaptive algorithm.

</details>


### [455] [ProRL: Effective Reinforcement Learning for Proactive Recommendation via Rectified Policy Gradient Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28293)
*Hongru Hou,Tiehua Mei,Denghui Geng,Jinhui Huang,Ao Xu,Hengrui Chen,Jiaqing Liang,Deqing Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes ProRL, a reinforcement learning framework for proactive recommender systems to efficiently optimize user recommendations by addressing gradient estimation deficiencies related to path-level reward structures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance proactive recommender systems by optimizing the decision-making process for recommending target items, focusing on avoiding biases in gradient estimation.

Method: Introduction of ProRL with two mechanisms: Stepwise Reward Centering to neutralize length-dependent bias and Position-Specific Advantage Estimation to reduce gradient variance.

Result: ProRL outperforms existing proactive recommender systems on three real-world datasets.

Conclusion: ProRL provides a more effective approach to recommendation generation, leveraging improved gradient estimation methods.

Abstract: Proactive Recommender Systems (PRSs) aim to guide user preference shift toward target items by generating paths of intermediate recommendations. Reinforcement learning (RL) provides a principled framework for optimizing such sequential decision tasks, as path rewards can naturally capture both short-term acceptance and long-term guidance effectiveness. However, naively applying policy gradients to PRS results in deficient gradient estimation.
